title: Nominative Pronouns
text: Approximate time: 30 minutes

title: Nominative Pronouns
text:
Aren’t these just pronouns?
While reading this you might be asking yourself, “Aren’t these just pronouns?” and “What does it mean to specifically be a nominative pronoun?” These are great questions and it is a distinction that we will be making early in these lessons. Let’s consider this sentence in English:

He eats her.

He and her are both pronouns in the above sentence, but He is the subject of the sentence while her is the thing that the verb is acting upon. We don’t say in English:

He eats she.

As you can see in English, our pronouns sometimes change based upon whether we are the doer of the action or the receiver of the action. This difference exists in German as well and is more nuanced. We will discuss this much more in later sections, but this is just to acknowledge that this difference exists so that we can demonstrate that not all pronouns are nominative, even in English.

title: Nominative Pronouns
section: What do we mean by formal and informal?
text:
What do we mean by formal and informal?
In German, when you are addressing certain people, you will use the formal version of you and other times you will use the informal version. Times you would consider using the formal version of you would be:

Speaking to someone you’ve never meet before
Speaking to someone of authority (police, teachers, etc.)
Speaking to someone significantly older than you as a sign of respect

The general rule that I use is:






NoteWhen to use the formal version of You



If a Southerner would use Sir or Ma’am to address this person, you’re probably using the formal version.



Moving from formal to informal
Some people, like police officers, you will always address with the formal version of You. However, like using Sir or Ma’am, someone may say something like “You don’t need to use Sir/Ma’am with me” and Germans have this same concept, but it is a bit more subtle. The person that commands respect will use the informal du to address you. At which point, you are now in a informal you relationship with this person for the rest of your life. The general rule for this is:






NoteWhen to transition from formal to informal



Once they use the informal version to address you, then you are now on an informal basis with this person for the rest of your life.




Differentiating between Sie and sie
You might notice from the above table that Sie (formal version of you (singular and plural)) is spelled the same as sie (third-person singular she and plural they). The pronunciation of these is the exact same and requires context to differentiate when speaking. The conjugation of the verb that follows can help with this context.
In written form the major distinction is that the the capitalized Sie refers to the formal version while the lowercase sie refers to either she or they. However, words at the start of a sentence are capitalized like in English and given that these are nominative pronouns, they are usually the subject of the sentence. As a result, it is common to see sie referring to she or they capitalized, but only because they start a sentence and not because they refer to the formal you.


Formal usage moving forward
We will limit the use of the formal you for simplicity moving forward in these lessons, but I will note it were applicable. This decision is to try to streamline examples and because, from my experience, learners oftentimes get a fair bit of latitude on this distinction. German speakers are generally excited to see you trying to speak German and not as focused on these social nuances. That being said, if I ran into a problem and needed help from the police or was meeting my partner’s parents/grandparents for the first time, I would try to give extra special attention toward using the formal version.






TipExercise



Make flashcards for these nominative pronouns and practice them until you feel confident with them.

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Regular
text:
ich mache
wir machen


du machst
ihr macht


er machtsie machtes macht
Sie machensie machen













ich spiele
wir spielen


du spielst
ihr spielt


er spieltsie spieltes spielt
Sie spielensie spielen













ich lerne
wir lernen


du lernst
ihr lernt


er lerntsie lerntes lernt
Sie lernensie lernen













ich frage
wir fragen


du fragst
ihr fragt


er fragtsie fragtes fragt
Sie fragensie fragen













ich sage
wir sagen


du sagst
ihr sagt


er sagtsie sagtes sagt
Sie sagensie sagen













ich kaufe
wir kaufen


du kaufst
ihr kauft


er kauftsie kauftes kauft
Sie kaufensie kaufen

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Exceptions
text: Approximate time: 45 minutes

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Exceptions
text:
Infinitive stems ending in -s, -ß, -x or -z
Infinitive verbs whose stem ends in -s, -ß, -x or -z only add a -t in the second-person singular. The rationale for this is that these infinitive stems already have either an -s sound at the end of the stem or a sound that is somewhat similar to -s. As a result, adding an -st ending wouldn’t be heard any different than -t. Some common words that fall into this category are:









-s
-ß
-x
-z




reisen 🔊 (to travel)
heißen 🔊 (to be named)
faxen 🔊 (to fax)
tanzen 🔊 (to dance)



schließen 🔊 (to close)
mixen 🔊 (to mix)
sitzen 🔊 (to sit)



grüßen 🔊 (to greet)

besitzen 🔊 (to own)









NoteRelation between -ß and -ss



This is probably a good place to point out that -s and -ß related. The -ß is sometimes replaced with -ss. As such, rules that apply to -s will also apply to -ß.


Let’s see an example of one of these conjugations:

heißen 🔊 (to be named)








ich heiße 🔊
wir heißen 🔊


du heißt 🔊
ihr heißt 🔊


er heißt 🔊sie heißtes heißt
Sie heißen 🔊sie heißen

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Exceptions
text:
Infinitive stems ending in -d or -t
Infinitive stems ending in -d or -t need to have an -e added after the infinitive stem but prior to the -st or -t in order to be able to pronounce it. Some common words that fall into this category are:







-d
-t




baden 🔊 (to bathe)
arbeiten 🔊 (to work)


schneiden 🔊 (to cut)
antworten 🔊 (to answer)



retten 🔊 (to rescue)



beten 🔊 (to pray)



Let’s see this conjugation in practice:

baden 🔊 (to bathe)








ich bade 🔊
wir baden 🔊


du badest 🔊
ihr badet 🔊


er badet 🔊 sie badet es badet
Sie baden 🔊 sie baden




arbeiten 🔊 (to work)








ich arbeite 🔊
wir arbeiten 🔊


du arbeitest 🔊
ihr arbeitet 🔊


er arbeitet 🔊 sie arbeitetes arbeitet
Sie arbeiten 🔊 sie arbeiten

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Exceptions
section: Infinitive stems ending in -m or -n preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r
text:
Infinitive stems ending in -m or -n preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r
Additionally, if the verb stem ends in an -m or -n that is preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r then we need to add an -e, just like the circumstances of infinitive stems ending with -d or -t above.
This rule can be a bit difficult for learners because it matters how one defines a “consonant sound”. Does the sound before the -m or -n sound like a vowel, -l or r? If so, it is treated normally. Otherwise, we need to add an -e. Let’s look at common application of this rule, then look at examples where we need to think about this “consonant sound” idea.

atmen 🔊 (to breathe)








ich atme 🔊
wir atmen 🔊


du atmest 🔊
ihr atmet 🔊


er atmet 🔊sie atmetes atmet
Sie atmen 🔊sie atmen




öffnen 🔊 (to open)








ich öffne 🔊
wir öffnen 🔊


du öffnest 🔊
ihr öffnet 🔊


er öffnet 🔊sie öffnetes öffnet
Sie öffnen 🔊sie öffnen



A table of common verbs that fall into the category of requiring an -e to be added are below:







-m preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r
-n preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r




atmen 🔊 (to breathe)
öffnen 🔊 (to open)



rechnen 🔊 (to calculate)



zeichnen 🔊 (to draw)



trocknen 🔊 (to dry (something))









NoteComment on the frequency of this category of verbs



There aren’t a ton of verbs that fall into this category. In fact, for the category of having an -m preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r, atmen 🔊, and it’s derivatives (einatmen 🔊 (to breathe in), ausatmen 🔊 (to breathe out), durchatmen 🔊 (to catch your breath), aufatmen 🔊 (to breathe a sigh of relief), etc.) is the only verb (that I am aware of) that falls into this category.
For infinitive stems with -n preceded by a consonant sound other than -l or -r, it is also quite limited. The table above covers all of the most common ones and a handful of others do exist (begegnen 🔊 (to meet (by chance)), entgegnen 🔊 (to reply (often contradictory)), segnen 🔊 (to bless), etc.), but they are quite infrequent to come across as a beginner.








NoteInfinitive stems ending in an -m or -n that are preceded by vowel sounds, -l or -r do not add an -e to the infinitive stem



To prove the rule, let’s look at a few infinitive stems that either end in -m or -n and are preceded by a vowel sound, -l or -r.

lernen 🔊 (to learn)








ich lerne 🔊
wir lernen 🔊


du lernst 🔊
ihr lernt 🔊


er lernt 🔊sie lerntes lernt
Sie lernen 🔊sie lernen




wärmen 🔊 (to warm)








ich wärme 🔊
wir wärmen 🔊


du wärmst 🔊
ihr wärmt 🔊


er wärmt 🔊sie wärmtes wärmt
Sie wärmen 🔊sie wärmen



Unlike the infinitive stems ending in -m or -n preceded by a consonant sound other than l or r, these are relatively more common. I have provided a list below of some of the more common ones you might come across:



beginnen 🔊 (to begin)
sich besinnen 🔊 (to recollect / to think better of)
brennen 🔊 (to burn)
dienen 🔊 (to serve (a cause))
entfernen 🔊 (to remove (something))
erwähnen 🔊 (to mention)
filmen 🔊 (to film)



sich gewöhnen* 🔊 (to become accustomed)
kennenlernen 🔊 (to get to know/to meet)
kommen 🔊 (to come)
lernen 🔊 (to learn)
meinen 🔊 (to mean)
wärmen 🔊 (to warm)
wohnen* 🔊 (to reside (somewhere))



* Note in these examples that the -n is preceded by an -h and you may be inclined to think that these are breaking the rule. However, in this context, the sound prior to the -n still has a “vowel-like” sound. This is part of the reason why this rule is the tricky.
I am also not aware of any circumstances where the infinitive stem has a -ln pattern and filmen is the only one I know with the -lm pattern.

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Regular
text:
Approximate time: 30 minutes

Learning Objectives
In this lesson, the learner will:

Conjugate regular verbs in the present tense



Conjugating verbs in the Present tense
Conjugation is how a verb changes based upon the doer of the action. In English, this looks like:

I jump
She jumps

The first-person singular version of “to jump” is jump, while the third-person singular version is jumps. In English, conjugation is usually pretty straightforward in many tenses. So much so, that we forget that we do it. However, many languages, including German, have more complexity in their conjugation and different subjects have different conjugations.
Let’s demonstrate conjugation of a regular verb in German, gehen, which means “to go”:







ich gehe
wir gehen


du gehst
ihr geht


er gehtsie gehtes geht
Sie* gehensie gehen



* Note that the formal Sie follows the conjugation of the third-person, plural, even though it refers to the second-person singular or plural. This will be true in all tenses.
For regular verbs the rule is to remove the -en ending from the infinitive version of the verb and add these endings for a given subject:



-e
-en


-st
-t


-t
-en









TipExercise



Let’s introduce a few more common regular verbs in the present tense.



Deutsch
English




machen
to do or to make


spielen
to play


lernen
to learn


fragen
to ask


sagen
to say


kaufen
to buy




Create flashcards to learn these common verbs
Conjugate each of the verbs

Answer Key

title: Present Tense Verb Conjugation - Irregular
text: Exercise
Code

Description
The goal of these materials are to provide a basic understanding of the German language for English-speakers where the explanations for the concepts and sentence structure will be provided in English. I should be clear from the start that my grasp of the German language is incomplete, but I am trying to write up lessons as my knowledge grows.
A major concept for language learning is vocabulary acquisition. In these materials, we are going to attempt to limit the vocabulary and rather focus on how the language itself works. At some points vocabulary will be necessary to progress, but I will be making attempts to minimize this as much as possible.

Learning Objectives
In these lessons, the learner will:

Describe grammar concepts from German
Assemble German sentences

A Note about General Rules
Throughout the materials we will be using “General Rules”. These are rules that hold in many, if not all, circumstances. We will denote them but putting them in a special block like:






NoteHow “General Rules” will look



“General Rules” will be in special blocks like this.
